review_summary: The transcript is sufficient for application-oriented questions about single-frame CNN baselines and the tradeoffs of late fusion without requiring a separate clip.

question_bank.question: Why is a single-frame CNN often a strong baseline for video classification?
choices: Many actions are already recognizable from appearance in sampled frames, and averaging frame predictions can work surprisingly well
It explicitly models long-range temporal dependencies better than recurrent models
It has more temporal shift invariance than 3D convolution
It always uses fewer training examples than clip-based models
question_bank.explanation: Some actions can be recognized from the posture, scene, or object configuration visible in individual frames. Averaging predictions across sampled frames can therefore perform well even without explicit motion modeling.

question_bank.question: What is the main drawback of late fusion by concatenating frame features before an MLP?
choices: The feature vector grows with the number of frames, which can create many parameters
It makes spatial features impossible to learn
It removes all temporal information before classification
It requires optical flow as an extra input
question_bank.explanation: Concatenation keeps separate information from each frame, but the resulting clip representation gets larger as more frames are used. A large input to the classifier can make the model parameter-heavy and inefficient.
